beats.narration_text: Hola — this is Liam, in for Bear. Ask Claude to add an app to the Cardputer, push a changed file without re-flashing, watch device serial logs, or run a one-shot REPL command — the Cardputer Buddy skill fires. It is the iterate-after-provisioning skill: m5-onboard handles the first install, this skill handles everything after. Let me walk you through the device layout, the launcher auto-discovery, and the three dev-loop scripts that drive all ongoing work.

props.command: Add a timer app to the Cardputer that counts down from 60 seconds.
output: Add file to buddy/device/apps/ → push → appears on next boot
Dev loop: install_apps.py · push.py · tail_serial.py · repl_run.py

beats.act: anatomy — device layout + launcher auto-discovery + template
beats.narration_text: The Cardputer runs MicroPython on a /flash/ filesystem. main.py is the launcher — it scans /flash/apps/ at boot and lists every dot-py file as a menu entry automatically. Drop a file into buddy/device/apps/, push it, and it appears on the next boot with no registration code. The payload has three zones: the launcher itself, the shared buddy libraries — BLE, UI, state, protocol, and character definitions — and the apps directory with the three bundled apps. The canonical template for new apps is hello_cardputer.py — the smallest working example covering keyboard polling, font rendering, and the exit convention. When you need to write a new app, start from that template.

props.sparkLine: Drop a .py in apps/. Push it. Appears on next boot. No registration needed.

beats.act: design — four dev-loop scripts + when to use each
beats.narration_text: Four scripts drive all ongoing development. install_apps.py pushes the full apps directory over USB-serial — use this when you have added or renamed files and need the complete directory in sync. push.py pushes a named subset of files — use this for a single changed file when you want a faster loop without touching unrelated files. These two are the most likely to be confused: install_apps.py for full sync, push.py for a targeted single-file push. tail_serial.py streams device logs — attach this in a second terminal while you test. repl_run.py runs a one-shot Python expression over the REPL — useful for probing the filesystem, checking state, or running a quick diagnostic without pushing a full file. PORT is whatever detect.py reported at provisioning time: /dev/cu.usbmodem on macOS, /dev/ttyACM on Linux, COM on Windows.

props.sparkLine: install_apps for full sync. push for one file. tail to watch. repl for one-shot probes.

beats.act: teardown — what it gets right / where it bites
beats.narration_text: Here is the teardown. Cardputer Buddy gets four things right. The trigger distinction is sharp: iterate-after-provisioning — m5-onboard handles the first install, this skill handles everything after. The /flash/ device layout is explicit with the full directory tree. The launcher auto-discovery — scan apps/ at boot, every dot-py is a menu entry — is an elegant convention that requires no registration code. The three dev-loop scripts are clearly separated by scope and mapped to concrete commands. Where it bites. PORT detection depends on detect.py from a previous provisioning run — if the port changes or the user is in a new session, there is no inline guidance for rediscovering it. push.py and install_apps.py are two different push mechanisms with overlapping scope — the distinction between them is buried and easy to get wrong, especially for a new developer who reaches for whichever one appears first. The BLE flow in claude_buddy.py is referenced but not documented: how Claude Desktop sends commands, what the protocol looks like, and what event triggers are available are not specified. And hello_cardputer.py is named as the canonical template but its required function signatures and exit conventions are not spelled out inline — you have to read the source.

props.sparkLine: Layout and dev-loop scripts solid. PORT gap and push-script ambiguity: surface them.

beats.narration_text: Verdict. Cardputer Buddy: iterate after provisioning — m5-onboard handles the first install. /flash/ layout: main.py launcher scans apps/ at boot — every dot-py is a menu entry. Template: hello_cardputer.py for keyboard, font, exit. Dev loop: install_apps.py full push, push.py single file, tail_serial.py for logs, repl_run.py for one-shot REPL. Gaps: PORT rediscovery on session change, install_apps vs push ambiguity, BLE protocol undocumented, hello_cardputer.py signatures not spelled out.

artifactLines: Iterate after provisioning — m5-onboard handles first install
/flash/apps/ auto-scanned at boot: every .py = menu entry, no registration
Template: hello_cardputer.py — keyboard polling · font · exit convention
install_apps.py: full sync · push.py: single file · tail_serial.py · repl_run.py
PORT: from detect.py at provisioning — /dev/cu.usbmodem · /dev/ttyACM · COM
Gaps: PORT gap · push/install_apps ambiguity · BLE undoc · template signatures missing

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Open a Claude Code session. Paste this: Add a timer app to the Cardputer that counts down from 60 seconds and returns to the launcher when done. Watch four things. First: does it create the new file in buddy/device/apps/, following the hello_cardputer.py structure? Second: does it use push.py for the single new file rather than install_apps.py for the full directory? Third: does it follow the exit convention — polling the keyboard for a key to return to the launcher — or does it hard-loop with no exit path? Fourth: after pushing, does it suggest running tail_serial.py to watch the output rather than requiring a re-flash to see logs?

props.command: Add a 60-second countdown timer app that returns to the launcher when done.
props.runningText: Watch: buddy/device/apps/? push.py not install_apps? exit key? tail_serial?
output: Expected: new .py in buddy/device/apps/ · push.py for single file
Expected: keyboard poll for exit key · tail_serial.py after push
Red flag: install_apps.py for one file · hard loop · no exit path

beats.narration_text: Cardputer Buddy. Liam, in for Bear.
